title: Fitting a plasterboard inspection hatch in a ceiling
subtitle: Cut an opening in a plasterboard ceiling and install a fire-rated or standard access hatch to reach loft services or pipework
excerpt: How to fit an inspection hatch in a plasterboard ceiling. Covers cutting the opening between joists, fitting the hatch frame, and finishing the surround flush with the existing ceiling so the hatch is discreet and neat.
sourceNotes: UK building trade practice. Reader's Digest Complete DIY Manual (referenced, not reproduced). Building Regulations Part B considerations for fire-rated hatches in ceiling membranes.

glossaryTerms.definition: A framed, removable panel cut into a ceiling to provide access to the void above (a loft space, ceiling plenum, or roof space. Standard sizes are 562 × 562 mm and 562 × 725 mm, sized to fit between standard 400 mm or 600 mm joist centres with noggins fitted to each end of the opening. Fire-rated hatches have intumescent seals and a fire-resisting door to maintain the ceiling's fire-resistance rating; standard hatches are non-fire-rated. Building Regulations Part B requires fire-rated hatches in certain ceiling locations.

content.text: gives access to the ceiling void for pipework, wiring, and insulation work. The approach is locating a pair of joists, cutting the opening in the plasterboard, fitting noggins at each end, and dropping a proprietary hatch frame into the opening. No plaster skimming required) the hatch frame covers the cut edge.
Locate joists and choose the position

Find two adjacent joists with a stud detector. The opening sits between them, with the hatch frame spanning the full clear width. Standard joists at 400 mm centres give a 350 to 380 mm clear span between faces; standard joists at 600 mm centres give 550 to 570 mm. Choose a hatch size that fits the joist spacing (avoid cutting a joist. Mark the opening position and double-check above the ceiling with a torch through a small test hole before cutting.

Fit a noggin) a short timber section cut from the same depth as the joist (between the two joists at each end of the hatch opening. Fix with 100 mm screws through the joist face into each end of the noggin. These noggins define the ends of the opening and support the hatch frame across the opening width.

Score the ceiling with a utility knife on the cut lines. Cut through the plasterboard with the jigsaw, supporting the cut-out piece with a free hand so it does not drop and split. The four cut lines should land tight against the inner faces of both joists and both noggins. Trim the opening square with the utility knife if the jigsaw line wanders.
Fit the hatch frame and lid

Push the hatch frame up into the opening. The frame flange sits against the ceiling face and the frame body clips or screws to the joists and noggins. Screw through the flange into the timber at each corner. Drop the plasterboard lid into the frame) most proprietary hatches use a simple drop-in panel held by gravity. Fill any gap between the frame flange and the ceiling with a flexible filler and sand flush when dry. Prime and paint to match.
